title: AI Risk Management Awareness
description: An awareness module covering the AI-specific risk landscape as addressed by ISO 23894, including bias and fairness risks, transparency and explainability, robustness and reliability, and accountability in AI systems.

sections.title: The AI-Specific Risk Landscape
sections.content:
Artificial intelligence systems present a distinct set of risks that extend beyond those associated with conventional information technology. ISO 23894 provides guidance on managing these risks within the broader framework of ISO 31000, recognising that AI introduces unique challenges related to data dependency, model complexity, and the potential for autonomous decision-making.

The AI risk landscape encompasses technical risks, such as model failure and data poisoning, as well as societal risks, including discrimination, loss of privacy, and erosion of human autonomy. These risks may manifest at any stage of the AI system lifecycle, from initial design and data collection through to deployment and ongoing operation.

Organisations deploying AI systems must consider the broader context in which those systems operate, including the regulatory environment, public expectations, and the potential for unintended consequences. The pace of technological change means that new risks may emerge rapidly, requiring organisations to maintain a dynamic and forward-looking approach to risk management.

Understanding the AI-specific risk landscape is a prerequisite for effective risk management. This module provides the foundational knowledge needed to identify, assess, and respond to the risks associated with AI, equipping participants with the awareness necessary to contribute to a responsible and risk-informed approach to AI adoption.

sections.title: Bias and Fairness Risks
sections.content:
Bias in AI systems is one of the most widely discussed and consequential risks associated with artificial intelligence. Bias can arise from multiple sources, including the data used to train models, the design choices made during development, and the way in which outputs are interpreted and acted upon by human users.

Training data bias occurs when the data used to develop an AI model does not accurately represent the population or context in which the model will be deployed. Historical data may reflect existing societal inequalities, leading the model to perpetuate or amplify discriminatory patterns. Selection bias, measurement bias, and label bias are all common forms of data-related bias that must be identified and addressed.

Algorithmic bias can emerge from the design of the model itself, including the choice of features, the optimisation objective, and the handling of underrepresented groups. Even when training data is carefully curated, design decisions can introduce or exacerbate unfairness. Organisations must adopt rigorous testing and validation practices to detect and mitigate algorithmic bias before deployment.

Fairness is not a single, universally agreed concept; different definitions of fairness may conflict with one another, and the appropriate definition will depend on the context and purpose of the AI system. Organisations should engage with affected stakeholders to understand the dimensions of fairness that are most relevant to their use case and should document the fairness criteria adopted, along with the rationale for their selection.

sections.title: Transparency and Explainability
sections.content:
Transparency and explainability are essential properties of trustworthy AI systems. Transparency refers to the degree to which the workings of an AI system — including its data, algorithms, and decision-making processes — are open to scrutiny by relevant stakeholders. Explainability relates to the ability to provide meaningful and understandable explanations of the system's outputs and behaviour.

ISO 23894 recognises that a lack of transparency can undermine trust in AI systems and make it difficult for organisations to identify and address risks. When the logic behind an AI decision is opaque, it is challenging to determine whether the system is operating correctly, fairly, and in accordance with organisational policies and legal requirements.

The level of transparency and explainability required will vary depending on the context and the potential impact of the AI system's decisions. High-stakes applications, such as those affecting individuals' rights, health, or financial wellbeing, typically demand a greater degree of explainability than low-risk, routine applications.

Organisations should establish clear expectations for transparency and explainability as part of their AI governance framework. This may include requirements for documentation of model design and training, the provision of explanations to affected individuals, and the availability of mechanisms for challenging AI-driven decisions. Investing in explainability techniques and tools supports both compliance and stakeholder confidence.

sections.title: Robustness and Reliability
sections.content:
Robustness and reliability are critical attributes of AI systems that organisations must address as part of their risk management activities. A robust AI system performs consistently and accurately under a range of conditions, including those that differ from the conditions encountered during training. Reliability refers to the system's ability to produce correct and repeatable outputs over time.

AI systems may be vulnerable to adversarial attacks, in which malicious actors deliberately manipulate inputs to cause the system to produce incorrect or harmful outputs. Adversarial robustness testing is an important element of the AI risk management process, helping organisations to identify and mitigate vulnerabilities before they can be exploited.

Model drift is another significant risk, occurring when the statistical properties of the data encountered in production diverge from those of the training data. Drift can lead to a gradual degradation of model performance, potentially resulting in inaccurate predictions or decisions. Organisations should implement monitoring mechanisms to detect drift and trigger retraining or recalibration as necessary.

Ensuring robustness and reliability requires a combination of rigorous development practices, comprehensive testing, and ongoing monitoring. Organisations should define performance thresholds and establish processes for responding to performance degradation. Regular validation against real-world data and conditions helps to maintain confidence in the system's outputs.

sections.title: Accountability in AI Systems
sections.content:
Accountability is a foundational principle of responsible AI deployment. It requires that clear roles and responsibilities are established for the development, deployment, and operation of AI systems, and that individuals and organisations can be held answerable for the outcomes produced by those systems.

ISO 23894 emphasises that accountability mechanisms should be embedded throughout the AI system lifecycle. This includes assigning ownership for key decisions — such as the selection of training data, the choice of model architecture, and the determination of deployment parameters — and ensuring that these decisions are documented and traceable.

Organisations should establish governance structures that provide oversight of AI activities, including the designation of senior individuals or committees with responsibility for AI risk management. These governance arrangements should be supported by policies, procedures, and reporting mechanisms that enable effective oversight and timely escalation of concerns.

Accountability also extends to the provision of redress for individuals adversely affected by AI-driven decisions. Organisations should implement accessible and effective mechanisms for reviewing and challenging AI outcomes, and should be transparent about the role of AI in decision-making processes. Building a culture of accountability supports trust, compliance, and the responsible use of artificial intelligence.

title: AI Risk Assessment & Treatment
description: A detailed module on conducting AI risk assessments and developing treatment strategies in accordance with ISO 23894, covering AI risk identification methodology, technical and societal risk analysis, evaluation criteria, treatment options, and continuous monitoring.

sections.title: AI Risk Identification Methodology
sections.content:
Identifying risks specific to AI systems requires a tailored methodology that accounts for the unique characteristics of these technologies. Whilst the general principles of risk identification from ISO 31000 apply, the complexity of AI systems — including their reliance on data, the opacity of certain algorithms, and the potential for emergent behaviours — demands additional techniques and considerations.

A structured AI risk identification process should consider risks across the entire system lifecycle, from data acquisition and model training through to deployment, operation, and decommissioning. At each stage, different types of risk may arise, and a lifecycle-based approach ensures that none are overlooked.

Techniques for AI risk identification include systematic review of the AI system architecture and data pipeline, threat modelling adapted for AI-specific attack vectors, analysis of historical incidents involving similar systems, and consultation with domain experts and affected stakeholders. Scenario analysis and red-teaming exercises can help to surface risks that may not be apparent through conventional assessment methods.

The output of the identification process should be a comprehensive AI risk register that captures each identified risk, its sources, the lifecycle stage at which it may manifest, and the potential consequences for the organisation and affected individuals. This register forms the basis for subsequent analysis and evaluation activities.

sections.title: AI Risk Analysis: Technical and Societal Dimensions
sections.content:
AI risk analysis involves developing a detailed understanding of each identified risk, encompassing both its technical and societal dimensions. Technical analysis focuses on the likelihood and impact of risks related to the AI system's performance, security, and reliability, whilst societal analysis considers the broader consequences for individuals, communities, and society.

Technical risk analysis for AI systems may involve evaluating model accuracy and error rates, assessing vulnerability to adversarial inputs, analysing the quality and representativeness of training data, and testing the system's behaviour under edge cases and stress conditions. Quantitative techniques, such as statistical testing and simulation, can provide rigorous estimates of technical risk levels.

Societal risk analysis addresses the potential for AI systems to cause harm beyond the immediate technical domain. This includes risks related to discrimination and inequality, loss of privacy, impacts on employment and economic opportunity, and effects on democratic processes and human autonomy. Assessing societal risks often requires qualitative methods, stakeholder engagement, and reference to ethical frameworks and human rights standards.

A comprehensive risk analysis integrates both technical and societal dimensions, recognising that they are often interconnected. For example, a technical failure in a healthcare AI system may have direct societal consequences for patient safety. Organisations should document the methodology, assumptions, and limitations of their analysis to ensure transparency and facilitate review.

sections.title: AI Risk Evaluation Criteria
sections.content:
Evaluating AI risks requires the establishment of criteria that reflect the specific nature and context of the AI system under assessment. These criteria should be defined before the risk analysis is undertaken and should be aligned with the organisation's objectives, values, and risk appetite.

AI risk evaluation criteria may include thresholds for model performance metrics, such as accuracy, precision, recall, and fairness measures. They may also encompass tolerance levels for societal impacts, regulatory compliance requirements, and stakeholder expectations. The criteria should be sufficiently specific to enable clear and consistent decision-making about which risks require treatment.

The evaluation process involves comparing the results of risk analysis against the established criteria to determine the significance of each risk. Risks that exceed the defined thresholds are prioritised for treatment, whilst those that fall within acceptable levels may be accepted and monitored. The evaluation should also consider the cumulative effect of multiple risks and the potential for risk interactions.

Organisations should involve relevant stakeholders in the definition and application of evaluation criteria, particularly where societal risks are concerned. Stakeholder perspectives can help to ensure that the criteria reflect a balanced and informed view of what constitutes acceptable risk. The criteria and the outcomes of the evaluation should be documented and communicated transparently.

sections.title: AI Risk Treatment Options
sections.content:
AI risk treatment involves selecting and implementing measures to modify identified risks to an acceptable level. The treatment options available for AI risks mirror those described in ISO 31000 — avoidance, reduction, sharing, and acceptance — but their application to AI systems involves specific considerations.

Risk avoidance may involve deciding not to develop or deploy an AI system where the risks are deemed unacceptable and cannot be adequately mitigated. This option should be considered where the potential for serious harm to individuals or society is high and where alternative, lower-risk approaches are available.

Risk reduction is the most common treatment approach for AI systems and may include measures such as improving training data quality, applying bias mitigation techniques, enhancing model validation and testing, implementing human oversight and intervention mechanisms, and strengthening security controls. The selection of reduction measures should be informed by the risk analysis and should be proportionate to the level of risk.

Risk sharing may involve transferring some of the risk to third parties, for example through insurance, contractual arrangements with AI service providers, or collaborative governance structures. Risk acceptance is appropriate where the residual risk falls within the organisation's risk appetite, provided that the decision is documented and subject to ongoing monitoring. Treatment plans should specify the actions, responsibilities, timescales, and success criteria for each measure.

sections.title: Continuous Monitoring of AI Risks
sections.content:
Continuous monitoring is essential to the effective management of AI risks, given the dynamic nature of AI systems and the environments in which they operate. Unlike static systems, AI models may change their behaviour over time as they encounter new data or as the underlying conditions shift, making ongoing vigilance a necessity.

Monitoring should encompass both the performance of the AI system and the effectiveness of the risk treatment measures in place. Key performance indicators (KPIs) and key risk indicators (KRIs) should be defined and tracked on a regular basis, with automated alerting where possible to enable timely response to emerging issues.

Organisations should establish processes for detecting and responding to model drift, data quality degradation, and changes in the external environment that may affect the risk profile of the AI system. Regular revalidation of the model against current data and conditions helps to ensure that performance remains within acceptable bounds.

The results of monitoring activities should be reported to relevant stakeholders, including risk owners, senior management, and, where appropriate, external regulators. Monitoring data should feed back into the risk management process, informing updates to the risk register, revisions to treatment plans, and improvements to the overall AI governance framework. A commitment to continuous monitoring demonstrates organisational responsibility and supports the long-term trustworthiness of AI systems.
